cards.front: What are the five red Bordeaux grapes permitted in AOC wines?
cards.back: Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Cabernet Franc, Petit Verdot, and Malbec (Cot).

cards.front: What are the three white Bordeaux grapes permitted in AOC wines?
cards.back: Sauvignon Blanc, Semillon, and Muscadelle.

cards.front: Which commune in the Haut-Medoc was elevated to its own AOC and is known for powerful, long-lived wines dominated by Cabernet Sauvignon?
cards.back: Pauillac. It is home to three of the five First Growths: Lafite Rothschild, Latour, and Mouton Rothschild.

cards.front: In the 1855 Classification of the Medoc, how many chateaux were classified as First Growths (Premiers Crus)?
cards.back: Four originally (Lafite Rothschild, Latour, Margaux, Haut-Brion); Mouton Rothschild was elevated from Second Growth in 1973, making five total.

cards.front: What is the dominant grape of Saint-Emilion?
cards.back: Merlot, often blended with Cabernet Franc. Cabernet Sauvignon plays a minor role.

cards.front: How is the Saint-Emilion classification different from the 1855 Medoc classification?
cards.back: The Saint-Emilion classification is revisited approximately every ten years (most recently 2022), whereas the 1855 classification has remained essentially unchanged since its creation.

cards.front: Name the two Premiers Grands Crus Classes 'A' of Saint-Emilion as of the 2012 classification.
cards.back: Chateau Ausone and Chateau Cheval Blanc. Chateau Angelus and Chateau Pavie were added in 2012.

cards.front: What is the primary grape variety of Pomerol?
cards.back: Merlot, often with Cabernet Franc. Unlike Saint-Emilion and the Medoc, Pomerol has no official classification system.

cards.front: What are the two main soil types on the Right Bank of Bordeaux, and which appellations are associated with each?
cards.back: Clay and limestone dominate in Saint-Emilion; clay and gravel dominate in Pomerol. These soils favor Merlot and Cabernet Franc.

cards.front: What is the key soil type of the Left Bank (Haut-Medoc) in Bordeaux?
cards.back: Deep gravel beds over clay and limestone, which provide excellent drainage and heat retention, favoring Cabernet Sauvignon.

cards.front: What sweet wine appellation lies within the Graves region of Bordeaux?
cards.back: Sauternes (and Barsac). Wines are made from Semillon, Sauvignon Blanc, and Muscadelle affected by Botrytis cinerea (noble rot).

cards.front: Name the six communal appellations of the Haut-Medoc.
cards.back: Saint-Estephe, Pauillac, Saint-Julien, Listrac-Medoc, Moulis-en-Medoc, and Margaux.

cards.front: What distinguishes Pessac-Leognan from the broader Graves AOC?
cards.back: Pessac-Leognan was carved out of northern Graves in 1987 and contains all of the Crus Classes de Graves. It produces both red and dry white wines.

cards.front: In Burgundy, what is the hierarchy of appellations from broadest to most specific?
cards.back: Regional (e.g., Bourgogne) -> Village (e.g., Gevrey-Chambertin) -> Premier Cru -> Grand Cru.

cards.front: How many Grand Cru vineyards exist in the Cote de Nuits?
cards.back: 24 Grand Crus, all producing red wine (except Musigny, which may produce a tiny amount of white).

cards.front: What grape is used for red Burgundy?
cards.back: Pinot Noir exclusively (with rare exceptions of Gamay permitted in Bourgogne Passetoutgrains).

cards.front: What grape is used for white Burgundy?
cards.back: Chardonnay, with small amounts of Aligote permitted in the Bourgogne Aligote appellation.

cards.front: Name the five major sub-regions of Burgundy from north to south.
cards.back: Chablis, Cote de Nuits, Cote de Beaune, Cote Chalonnaise, and Maconnais.

cards.front: What is the largest Grand Cru vineyard in the Cote de Nuits?
cards.back: Clos de Vougeot, at approximately 50 hectares, with over 80 individual owners.

cards.front: Name the Grand Cru vineyards of Gevrey-Chambertin.
cards.back: Chambertin, Chambertin-Clos de Beze, Charmes-Chambertin, Mazoyeres-Chambertin, Latricieres-Chambertin, Griotte-Chambertin, Ruchottes-Chambertin, Chapelle-Chambertin, and Mazis-Chambertin (9 total).

cards.front: What is unique about the Grand Cru Corton in the Cote de Beaune?
cards.back: Corton is the only red Grand Cru in the Cote de Beaune. The hill of Corton also produces Corton-Charlemagne (white Grand Cru).

cards.front: What are the two white Grand Crus of the Cote de Beaune?
cards.back: Montrachet and Corton-Charlemagne. (Montrachet also has hyphenated Grand Crus: Chevalier-Montrachet, Batard-Montrachet, Bienvenues-Batard-Montrachet, Criots-Batard-Montrachet.)

cards.front: How many Grand Cru vineyards does Chablis have, and what are they?
cards.back: Seven: Blanchot, Bougros, Les Clos, Grenouilles, Les Preuses, Valmur, and Vaudesir. All are Chardonnay.

cards.front: What soil type defines Chablis and gives its wines a distinctive mineral character?
cards.back: Kimmeridgian limestone and marl, composed of ancient oyster-shell fossils.

cards.front: What is Beaujolais's grape variety, and which appellation produces the most age-worthy wines?
cards.back: Gamay. The ten Beaujolais Crus (e.g., Morgon, Moulin-a-Vent, Fleurie) produce the most structured, age-worthy wines.

cards.front: Name all ten Beaujolais Crus.
cards.back: Brouilly, Cote de Brouilly, Chenas, Chiroubles, Fleurie, Julienas, Morgon, Moulin-a-Vent, Regnie, and Saint-Amour.

cards.front: What is the primary winemaking technique associated with Beaujolais?
cards.back: Carbonic maceration (whole-cluster fermentation in a CO2-rich environment), producing fruity, low-tannin wines.

cards.front: What are the three main grape varieties of the Northern Rhone?
cards.back: Syrah (red), Viognier, Marsanne, and Roussanne (white). Syrah is the sole red grape; the whites may be co-fermented in some appellations.

cards.front: Name the key Northern Rhone appellations for red wine.
cards.back: Cote-Rotie, Hermitage, Cornas, Saint-Joseph, and Crozes-Hermitage. All are 100% Syrah (Cote-Rotie may include up to 20% Viognier).

cards.front: What distinguishes the two hillsides of Cote-Rotie?
cards.back: Cote Brune (darker, mica-rich schist soil, fuller wines) and Cote Blonde (lighter, gneiss and limestone soil, more elegant wines).

cards.front: What is Condrieu known for?
cards.back: White wines made exclusively from Viognier. It is the most prestigious appellation for this grape variety.

cards.front: What is the dominant grape blend in Chateauneuf-du-Pape?
cards.back: Grenache-based, though 13 grape varieties (18 counting color variations) are permitted. Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvedre form the backbone of most blends.

cards.front: What distinguishes the soils of Chateauneuf-du-Pape?
cards.back: Large, round, heat-retaining stones called galets roules (rolled pebbles) cover many vineyards, reflecting heat onto the vines.

cards.front: What are the three styles of Champagne based on grape composition?
cards.back: Blanc de Blancs (100% Chardonnay), Blanc de Noirs (Pinot Noir and/or Pinot Meunier), and Rose (blended or saignee method).

cards.front: What are the three permitted grape varieties in Champagne?
cards.back: Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier (Meunier). Small amounts of Arbane, Petit Meslier, Pinot Blanc, and Pinot Gris are also allowed.

cards.front: What is the minimum aging requirement for non-vintage Champagne on lees?
cards.back: 15 months on the lees, with at least 12 months after tirage (bottling with yeast and sugar for secondary fermentation).

cards.front: What is the minimum aging requirement for vintage Champagne on lees?
cards.back: 36 months on the lees.

cards.front: Explain dosage in Champagne production.
cards.back: Dosage is the addition of a liqueur d'expedition (wine and sugar mixture) after disgorgement. It determines sweetness level: Brut Nature (0-3 g/L), Extra Brut (0-6 g/L), Brut (0-12 g/L), Extra Dry/Sec (12-17 g/L), Sec (17-32 g/L), Demi-Sec (32-50 g/L), Doux (50+ g/L).

cards.front: What are the main sub-regions of Champagne?
cards.back: Montagne de Reims (Pinot Noir), Cote des Blancs (Chardonnay), Vallee de la Marne (Pinot Meunier), Cote des Bar/Aube (Pinot Noir).

cards.front: What does 'Grand Cru' mean in Champagne?
cards.back: Villages rated 100% on the old Echelle des Crus system. There are 17 Grand Cru villages. Premier Cru villages were rated 90-99%.

cards.front: What grape varieties dominate Alsace?
cards.back: Riesling, Gewurztraminer, Pinot Gris, and Muscat are the four 'noble' grapes permitted for Grand Cru and Vendange Tardive/Selection de Grains Nobles wines. Pinot Blanc, Sylvaner, and Pinot Noir are also grown.

cards.front: What do Vendange Tardive and Selection de Grains Nobles mean in Alsace?
cards.back: Vendange Tardive (VT) = late harvest with higher minimum sugar levels. Selection de Grains Nobles (SGN) = individually selected botrytized grapes with even higher sugar requirements. Both must be from noble varieties.

cards.front: What are the main grape varieties of the Loire Valley from east to west?
cards.back: Sauvignon Blanc (Sancerre, Pouilly-Fume), Chenin Blanc (Vouvray, Savennieres), Cabernet Franc (Chinon, Bourgueil, Saumur-Champigny), and Melon de Bourgogne (Muscadet).

cards.front: What is sur lie aging, and where is it most associated in France?
cards.back: Aging wine on its spent yeast (lees) without racking, adding texture and complexity. Most associated with Muscadet sur Lie in the Loire Valley.

cards.front: What styles of wine does Vouvray produce?
cards.back: Sec (dry), demi-sec (off-dry), moelleux (sweet), and sparkling (methode traditionnelle), all from 100% Chenin Blanc.

cards.front: What is Savennieres known for?
cards.back: Dry, full-bodied, age-worthy white wines from Chenin Blanc. Coulee de Serrant is a famous single-vineyard monopole within the appellation.

cards.front: What is the primary grape of Sancerre and Pouilly-Fume?
cards.back: Sauvignon Blanc. Both appellations sit on the eastern end of the Loire Valley, known for flinty, mineral-driven whites.

cards.front: What are the four levels of the French AOC/AOP system?
cards.back: Vin de France (table wine), IGP (Indication Geographique Protegee), AOC/AOP (Appellation d'Origine Controlee/Protegee). Within AOC, there may be hierarchy (Village, Premier Cru, Grand Cru in Burgundy).

cards.front: What is the DOCG designation in Italy?
cards.back: Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita — the highest level of Italian wine classification, requiring stricter production standards and government tasting approval.

cards.front: What are the four levels of the Italian wine classification system?
cards.back: Vino (table wine), IGT (Indicazione Geografica Tipica), DOC (Denominazione di Origine Controllata), DOCG (highest).

cards.front: What grape makes Barolo and Barbaresco?
cards.back: Nebbiolo. Both are DOCG wines from Piedmont. Barolo requires minimum 38 months aging (18 in wood); Barbaresco requires 26 months (9 in wood).

cards.front: What are the key differences between Barolo and Barbaresco?
cards.back: Both are 100% Nebbiolo. Barolo has longer minimum aging (38 vs 26 months), tends to be more powerful and tannic, and comes from vineyards around the communes of Barolo, La Morra, Castiglione Falletto, Serralunga d'Alba, and Monforte d'Alba. Barbaresco is often more elegant and approachable younger.

cards.front: What grape is Barbera d'Asti made from?
cards.back: Barbera. It is Piedmont's most widely planted red grape, producing medium-bodied wines with high acidity and low tannin.

cards.front: What is Brunello di Montalcino made from?
cards.back: 100% Sangiovese (locally called Brunello). DOCG requires minimum 5 years aging (2 in oak) before release; Riserva requires 6 years.

cards.front: What is Chianti Classico, and what grape dominates it?
cards.back: Chianti Classico is a DOCG in central Tuscany (between Florence and Siena). It must contain at least 80% Sangiovese. Gran Selezione is the top tier, introduced in 2014.

cards.front: What are the three tiers of Chianti Classico?
cards.back: Chianti Classico (annata), Chianti Classico Riserva (minimum 24 months aging), and Chianti Classico Gran Selezione (minimum 30 months aging, from estate-grown grapes).

cards.front: What is Vino Nobile di Montepulciano?
cards.back: A DOCG Tuscan red made from at least 70% Sangiovese (locally called Prugnolo Gentile). Not to be confused with Montepulciano d'Abruzzo, which is a different grape.

cards.front: What are 'Super Tuscans'?
cards.back: Premium Tuscan wines that historically fell outside DOC regulations by using non-traditional grape varieties (especially Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot) or blends. Many were labeled as IGT. Examples: Sassicaia, Tignanello, Ornellaia.

cards.front: What is the Bolgheri DOC known for?
cards.back: Bordeaux-style red blends on the Tuscan coast. Sassicaia (Cabernet Sauvignon/Cabernet Franc) has its own sub-appellation, Bolgheri Sassicaia DOC.

cards.front: What grape variety is Amarone della Valpolicella made from?
cards.back: A blend of Corvina (45-95%), Corvinone, and Rondinella. Grapes are dried (appassimento) for several months before fermentation, concentrating sugars, yielding a rich, high-alcohol dry wine.

cards.front: What is Ripasso della Valpolicella?
cards.back: A Valpolicella wine re-fermented on the leftover grape skins (pomace) from Amarone production, adding body, richness, and complexity.

cards.front: What is Prosecco, and where is it produced?
cards.back: A sparkling wine made from Glera grapes using the Charmat (tank) method. DOC Prosecco covers a large area in Veneto and Friuli. Conegliano Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore is DOCG.

cards.front: What is Franciacorta?
cards.back: Italy's premier traditional method (metodo classico) sparkling wine DOCG, from Lombardy. Made from Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Blanc.

cards.front: What grape is Soave made from?
cards.back: Garganega (minimum 70%) in the Veneto region. Soave Classico comes from the original hillside zone.

cards.front: What is Verdicchio, and where is it grown?
cards.back: A white grape grown in the Marche region (central Italy), producing crisp, almond-noted whites. Key DOCs: Verdicchio dei Castelli di Jesi and Verdicchio di Matelica.

cards.front: What are the primary grape varieties of Etna DOC in Sicily?
cards.back: Nerello Mascalese (red) and Carricante (white), grown on volcanic soils on the slopes of Mount Etna.

cards.front: What is Primitivo, and what is its connection to the New World?
cards.back: Primitivo is grown primarily in Puglia (southern Italy). It is genetically identical to Zinfandel, which originated in Croatia as Crljenak Kastelanski/Tribidrag.

cards.front: What is Lambrusco?
cards.back: A family of red grape varieties from Emilia-Romagna producing sparkling (frizzante) red wines in dry to sweet styles. Usually made by the Charmat method.

cards.front: Name the three primary grapes of Aglianico-based DOCG wines in southern Italy.
cards.back: Aglianico is the sole primary grape. The key DOCGs are Taurasi (Campania) and Aglianico del Vulture Superiore (Basilicata), both producing powerful, tannic reds.

cards.front: What is the Denominazione di Origine of Moscato d'Asti?
cards.back: DOCG. Made from Moscato Bianco (Muscat Blanc a Petits Grains) in Piedmont. It is a slightly sparkling (frizzante), low-alcohol (5-6.5% ABV), sweet wine.

cards.front: What is Sagrantino di Montefalco?
cards.back: A DOCG red wine from Umbria made from 100% Sagrantino, known for extremely high tannins. Produced in both dry (secco) and sweet passito styles.

cards.front: What are the main wine regions of Spain's classification system?
cards.back: Vino de Mesa, Vino de la Tierra (VdlT/IGP), DO (Denominacion de Origen), DOCa/DOQ (highest: only Rioja and Priorat hold this status).

cards.front: What are the three sub-regions of Rioja?
cards.back: Rioja Alta (higher elevation, elegant wines), Rioja Alavesa (Basque influence, similar to Alta), and Rioja Oriental (formerly Rioja Baja, warmer, riper, Garnacha-dominant).

cards.front: What are the aging classifications for Rioja wines?
cards.back: Joven (little/no oak), Crianza (min 2 years, 1 in oak), Reserva (min 3 years, 1 in oak), Gran Reserva (min 5 years, 2 in oak). For whites: shorter oak requirements.

cards.front: What is the primary grape of Rioja?
cards.back: Tempranillo, often blended with Garnacha, Graciano, and Mazuelo (Carignan).

cards.front: What grape dominates Ribera del Duero?
cards.back: Tempranillo (locally called Tinto Fino or Tinta del Pais). Wines must be at least 75% Tempranillo.

cards.front: What is Priorat, and why is it significant?
cards.back: A DOCa in Catalonia known for old-vine Garnacha and Carignan (Carinyena) grown on llicorella (slate) soils. One of only two DOCa regions in Spain (with Rioja).

cards.front: What are the main types of Sherry?
cards.back: Fino (light, dry, aged under flor), Manzanilla (Fino from Sanlucar de Barrameda), Amontillado (aged under flor then oxidatively), Oloroso (fully oxidative aging), Palo Cortado (rare, characteristics of both), Cream and PX (sweet styles).

cards.front: What grape is Sherry made from?
cards.back: Palomino Fino for dry styles; Pedro Ximenez (PX) and Moscatel for sweet styles. Produced in the 'Sherry Triangle' of Jerez de la Frontera, Sanlucar de Barrameda, and El Puerto de Santa Maria.

cards.front: What is the solera system?
cards.back: A fractional blending system used in Sherry production. Wine is drawn from the oldest barrels (solera) and replenished from younger barrels (criaderas) above, ensuring consistency and complexity.

cards.front: What is flor in Sherry production?
cards.back: A film of yeast (primarily Saccharomyces) that forms on the surface of wine in partially filled barrels, protecting it from oxidation and contributing nutty, brioche-like flavors. Essential for Fino and Manzanilla styles.

cards.front: What is Cava, and how is it made?
cards.back: Spanish sparkling wine made by the traditional method (metodo tradicional). Primary grapes: Macabeo, Parellada, and Xarel-lo. Most is produced in Penedes, Catalonia.

cards.front: What is Albarino, and where is it primarily grown in Spain?
cards.back: A white grape grown in Rias Baixas (Galicia, northwest Spain). Produces aromatic, crisp, often saline whites.

cards.front: What is Garnacha/Grenache's role in Spanish winemaking?
cards.back: Spain's most widely planted red grape. Key in Priorat, Rioja (blending), Navarra, Campo de Borja, and Calatayud. Produces fruity, medium-tannin wines and also rose.

cards.front: What is Manzanilla Sherry?
cards.back: A Fino-style Sherry that can only be aged in Sanlucar de Barrameda. The coastal humidity produces thicker flor, resulting in a lighter, more saline character than Jerez Fino.

cards.front: What grape is Verdejo associated with in Spain?
cards.back: Verdejo is the primary grape of Rueda DO in Castilla y Leon. It produces aromatic, herbal, citrus-driven white wines.

cards.front: What is Monastrell, and where is it grown?
cards.back: Monastrell (Mourvedre) is widely grown in southeastern Spain, especially Jumilla and Yecla DOs. Produces full-bodied, dark, tannic reds.

cards.front: What is Txakoli?
cards.back: A slightly sparkling, high-acid, low-alcohol white wine from the Basque Country (Getariako Txakolina DO). Primary grape: Hondarrabi Zuri.

cards.front: What is the Vinos de Pago classification in Spain?
cards.back: Single-estate wines from recognized high-quality vineyards. A Pago is essentially a named vineyard estate that has its own DO-level classification — the highest recognition a single estate can achieve.

cards.front: What is the German wine classification system based on?
cards.back: Traditionally based on must weight (ripeness at harvest): Kabinett, Spatlese, Auslese, Beerenauslese (BA), Trockenbeerenauslese (TBA), Eiswein. The newer VDP classification emphasizes vineyard sites (Gutswein, Ortswein, Erste Lage, Grosse Lage).

cards.front: What is the difference between Kabinett and Spatlese in German wine?
cards.back: Both are Pradikatswein levels. Kabinett is the lightest, from normally ripe grapes. Spatlese ('late harvest') requires higher must weight/ripeness. Both can be made trocken (dry) or with residual sugar.

cards.front: What are the primary grape varieties of Germany?
cards.back: Riesling (most prestigious), Muller-Thurgau (most planted white), Spatburgunder (Pinot Noir, most planted red), Grauburgunder (Pinot Gris), Weissburgunder (Pinot Blanc), Silvaner.

cards.front: Name the 13 Anbaugebiete (wine regions) of Germany.
cards.back: Ahr, Baden, Franken, Hessische Bergstrasse, Mittelrhein, Mosel, Nahe, Pfalz, Rheingau, Rheinhessen, Saale-Unstrut, Sachsen, and Wurttemberg.

cards.front: What is the Mosel known for?
cards.back: Steep slate-soiled vineyards producing elegant, racy Rieslings with high acidity and often moderate alcohol. Key villages: Bernkastel, Piesport, Wehlen, Urzig.

cards.front: What is the Rheingau known for?
cards.back: Premium Riesling (and some Spatburgunder) from south-facing slopes along the Rhine River. Key estates: Schloss Johannisberg, Robert Weil. Also home to Kloster Eberbach.

cards.front: What is the VDP (Verband Deutscher Pradikatsweinguter)?
cards.back: An association of approximately 200 top German estates with a vineyard classification system: Gutswein (regional), Ortswein (village), Erste Lage (premier cru), Grosse Lage (grand cru). Grosse Gewachs (GG) denotes dry wines from Grosse Lage sites.

cards.front: What is Trockenbeerenauslese (TBA)?
cards.back: The highest Pradikat level in Germany/Austria. Made from individually selected berries affected by noble rot (Botrytis cinerea), producing extremely sweet, concentrated, expensive wines.

cards.front: What is Eiswein?
cards.back: Wine made from grapes naturally frozen on the vine (harvested at -7C/19.4F or below). Must weight must equal at least Beerenauslese level. Produces intensely sweet, high-acid wines.

cards.front: What is Gruner Veltliner?
cards.back: Austria's signature white grape, producing wines ranging from light and peppery to full-bodied and age-worthy. Predominant in Niederosterreich (Lower Austria), especially Wachau, Kremstal, and Kamptal.

cards.front: What are the three Wachau wine categories?
cards.back: Steinfeder (light, up to 11.5% ABV), Federspiel (medium, 11.5-12.5%), and Smaragd (full-bodied, 12.5%+). These are classification terms of the Vinea Wachau association.

cards.front: What is Sekt?
cards.back: German sparkling wine. Deutscher Sekt is made from German-grown grapes. It can be made by traditional method, transfer method, or Charmat. Winzersekt denotes estate-produced traditional method sparkling.

cards.front: What is Franken known for?
cards.back: Silvaner-based dry wines from northern Bavaria. Wines are traditionally bottled in a flat, round Bocksbeutel flask.

cards.front: What is the Austrian DAC system?
cards.back: Districtus Austriae Controllatus — Austria's appellation system designating wines of specific regional typicity. Examples: Weinviertel DAC (Gruner Veltliner), Kamptal DAC (Gruner Veltliner and Riesling).

cards.front: What is Blaufrankisch?
cards.back: Austria's premier red grape (known as Lemberger in Germany, Kekfrankos in Hungary). Produces medium to full-bodied wines with firm tannins and spice. Key region: Burgenland (especially Mittelburgenland).

cards.front: What is Pfalz known for?
cards.back: Germany's second-largest and warmest major region. Known for full-bodied, dry Riesling and excellent Spatburgunder (Pinot Noir). Lies adjacent to Alsace.

cards.front: What is the primary grape of the Napa Valley?
cards.back: Cabernet Sauvignon. Napa Valley is an AVA within which there are 16 nested sub-AVAs, including Oakville, Rutherford, Stags Leap District, and Howell Mountain.

cards.front: What distinguishes the Stags Leap District AVA?
cards.back: Known for elegant, supple Cabernet Sauvignon. Located on the eastern benchland of Napa Valley. A 1976 Paris Tasting ('Judgment of Paris') winner, Stag's Leap Wine Cellars, comes from here.

cards.front: What is the Rutherford Bench known for?
cards.back: An area within Rutherford AVA (Napa Valley) known for alluvial, well-drained soils producing Cabernet Sauvignon with characteristic 'Rutherford Dust' — a distinctive mineral/cocoa quality.

cards.front: What is the primary grape of Sonoma's Russian River Valley AVA?
cards.back: Pinot Noir and Chardonnay dominate, benefiting from fog and cool marine influence from the Pacific through the Petaluma Gap.

cards.front: What is the primary grape and style of Dry Creek Valley AVA in Sonoma?
cards.back: Known for old-vine Zinfandel, producing bold, spicy, berry-driven reds. Also produces excellent Sauvignon Blanc.

cards.front: What is the Willamette Valley known for?
cards.back: Oregon's primary wine region, located south of Portland. Known for cool-climate Pinot Noir and Pinot Gris. Contains sub-AVAs such as Dundee Hills, Eola-Amity Hills, and Chehalem Mountains.

cards.front: What is the primary grape of Argentina, and in which region is it most prominent?
cards.back: Malbec, centered in Mendoza (especially the Uco Valley and Lujan de Cuyo sub-regions). Argentina is the world's largest producer of Malbec.

cards.front: What white grape is Argentina known for?
cards.back: Torrontes, an aromatic grape primarily grown in the Salta region (high-altitude Cafayate Valley). Produces floral, Muscat-like dry whites.

cards.front: What is Chile's most planted red grape?
cards.back: Cabernet Sauvignon, followed by Pais (Mission grape) and Merlot. Chile also has significant plantings of Carmenere, once thought to be Merlot.

cards.front: What is Carmenere's significance in Chile?
cards.back: A Bordeaux variety nearly wiped out by phylloxera in France. It was rediscovered in Chilean vineyards in 1994, having been confused with Merlot. Now considered Chile's signature red grape.

cards.front: Name the key wine regions of Chile from north to south.
cards.back: Elqui/Limari (cool, northern), Aconcagua (warm), Casablanca/San Antonio (cool, coastal), Maipo (Cab Sauv), Rapel/Colchagua (reds), Curico, Maule (bulk and quality), Bio-Bio/Itata (southern cool climate).

cards.front: What are Australia's key wine regions and their specialties?
cards.back: Barossa Valley (Shiraz), Eden Valley (Riesling), Clare Valley (Riesling), McLaren Vale (Shiraz, Grenache), Coonawarra (Cabernet Sauvignon on terra rossa), Margaret River (Bordeaux blends, Chardonnay), Hunter Valley (Semillon, Shiraz), Yarra Valley (Pinot Noir, Chardonnay).

cards.front: What soil type defines Coonawarra in South Australia?
cards.back: Terra rossa — a thin layer of red loam over limestone. Known for producing structured, age-worthy Cabernet Sauvignon.

cards.front: What is the most planted grape in New Zealand?
cards.back: Sauvignon Blanc, with Marlborough (South Island) as the dominant region, known for intensely aromatic, herbaceous wines.

cards.front: What other regions and grapes is New Zealand known for?
cards.back: Central Otago (southernmost, Pinot Noir), Martinborough (Pinot Noir), Hawke's Bay (Bordeaux blends, Syrah), Waipara/Canterbury (Pinot Noir, Riesling).

cards.front: What is South Africa's most distinctive grape variety?
cards.back: Pinotage — a cross between Pinot Noir and Cinsault (Hermitage), created in 1925 by Abraham Perold at the University of Stellenbosch.

cards.front: What is the Wine of Origin (WO) system in South Africa?
cards.back: South Africa's appellation system guaranteeing origin, vintage, and cultivar. Key regions: Stellenbosch, Paarl, Franschhoek, Swartland, Constantia, Walker Bay.

cards.front: What is the Swartland region of South Africa known for?
cards.back: Old-vine Chenin Blanc, Syrah, and Rhone-style blends. It has become South Africa's most dynamic quality-focused wine region.

cards.front: What are the AVA requirements in the United States?
cards.back: To use an AVA name, 85% of grapes must come from that AVA. For varietal labeling, 75% of the wine must be from the named grape. Vintage dating requires 95% from the stated vintage.

cards.front: What is Washington State known for in wine production?
cards.back: Second-largest wine-producing state in the US. Columbia Valley AVA dominates. Key sub-AVAs: Walla Walla, Red Mountain, Yakima Valley, Horse Heaven Hills. Known for Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, Syrah, and Riesling.

cards.front: What makes Red Mountain AVA in Washington unique?
cards.back: Washington's warmest AVA, known for intensely concentrated Cabernet Sauvignon and Syrah. Low rainfall and warm days with cool nights produce small berries with thick skins.

cards.front: What is the Finger Lakes region known for?
cards.back: New York State's premier wine region. Known for high-quality Riesling grown around deep glacial lakes that moderate temperatures.

cards.front: What grape varieties define the Santa Rita Hills AVA?
cards.back: Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. Located in Santa Barbara County, California, the east-west oriented valleys channel Pacific Ocean fog and wind, creating a very cool climate.

cards.front: What is Paso Robles known for?
cards.back: A large AVA in San Luis Obispo County, California. Known for Rhone varieties (Syrah, Grenache, Mourvedre), Zinfandel, and Cabernet Sauvignon. Wide diurnal temperature shifts.

cards.front: What is the significance of the Judgment of Paris (1976)?
cards.back: A blind tasting organized by Steven Spurrier in which California wines (Stag's Leap Wine Cellars Cab Sauv and Chateau Montelena Chardonnay) defeated top French wines, bringing international attention to California wine.

cards.front: What is Chardonnay's role in Burgundy vs. California?
cards.back: In Burgundy, Chardonnay reflects terroir with restrained use of oak. In California, styles range from rich, buttery, oaky (Napa, Sonoma) to leaner, more mineral expressions (Sonoma Coast, Santa Barbara).

cards.front: What are the flavor characteristics of Cabernet Sauvignon?
cards.back: Black currant (cassis), blackberry, cedar, tobacco, green bell pepper (when underripe). Full-bodied, high tannin, high acidity, ages well.

cards.front: What are the primary flavor characteristics of Pinot Noir?
cards.back: Red cherry, raspberry, strawberry, earth, mushroom, game (with age). Light to medium body, moderate tannin, high acidity. Transparent expression of terroir.

cards.front: What are the primary flavor characteristics of Syrah/Shiraz?
cards.back: Dark fruit, black pepper, smoked meat, violet, olive. Full body, moderate to high tannin. Cool-climate Syrah is more peppery and savory; warm-climate Shiraz is riper and fruitier.

cards.front: Describe the key characteristics of Riesling.
cards.back: Highly aromatic: citrus, stone fruit, petrol (with age), floral. High acidity, light to medium body. Ranges from bone-dry to lusciously sweet. One of the most age-worthy white varieties.

cards.front: What are the primary characteristics of Sauvignon Blanc?
cards.back: Herbaceous, citrus, gooseberry, grass, bell pepper, tropical fruit. High acidity, light to medium body. Typically unoaked (Loire, NZ) but sometimes oak-aged (Bordeaux blends, some California 'Fume Blanc').

cards.front: What grape is Viognier, and what are its key characteristics?
cards.back: An aromatic white grape with stone fruit (peach, apricot), floral (honeysuckle, violet) character. Full-bodied, low to moderate acidity. Key in Condrieu (Rhone) and increasingly in California and Australia.

cards.front: What is Grenache/Garnacha's profile?
cards.back: Red fruit (strawberry, raspberry), spice, white pepper. Medium to full body, high alcohol potential, moderate tannin, moderate acidity. Often blended (GSM: Grenache-Syrah-Mourvedre) or as Chateauneuf-du-Pape, Priorat.

cards.front: Describe Tempranillo's key characteristics.
cards.back: Cherry, plum, leather, tobacco, vanilla (from oak aging). Medium body, moderate acidity and tannin. Spain's most important red grape. Also known as Tinto Fino, Tinta Roriz (Portugal), Cencibel.

cards.front: What is Mourvedre/Monastrell?
cards.back: Dark-skinned grape producing tannic, meaty, dark-fruited wines. Needs warmth to ripen fully. Key in Bandol (Provence), southern Rhone blends, and Jumilla (Spain).

cards.front: What is the Sangiovese grape known for?
cards.back: Red cherry, tomato, herbs, leather, earth. High acidity, high tannin, medium body. Italy's most widely planted red grape. Key in Chianti, Brunello di Montalcino, Vino Nobile di Montepulciano.

cards.front: What is Nebbiolo's flavor profile?
cards.back: Rose, tar, cherry, truffle, leather. High tannin, high acidity despite pale color. Age-worthy. Found primarily in Piedmont (Barolo, Barbaresco, Roero, Gattinara, Ghemme).

cards.front: What is Gewurztraminer?
cards.back: An aromatic white grape with intense lychee, rose petal, ginger, and spice aromas. Full-bodied, low acidity, high alcohol. Key in Alsace; also grown in Germany, Alto Adige, and New Zealand.

cards.front: What is Chenin Blanc's range of styles?
cards.back: Extremely versatile: dry (Savennieres), off-dry (Vouvray Demi-Sec), sweet (Coteaux du Layon, Bonnezeaux), sparkling (Cremant de Loire, Vouvray Mousseux). Flavors: apple, quince, honey, lanolin, wet wool.

cards.front: What is Merlot's flavor profile and key regions?
cards.back: Plum, black cherry, chocolate, herbal notes. Medium to full body, soft tannins, moderate acidity. Key in Bordeaux Right Bank (Pomerol, Saint-Emilion), Washington State, Chile, and northern Italy.

cards.front: Describe Malbec's characteristics.
cards.back: Dark-skinned grape producing inky, full-bodied wines with plum, violet, and dark fruit flavors. Originally from Cahors (SW France) where it's called Cot. Now Argentina's signature variety, especially at high altitudes in Mendoza.

cards.front: What is Cabernet Franc's profile?
cards.back: Red fruit, violet, graphite, green pepper, tobacco. Medium body, moderate tannin, higher acidity than Cab Sauv. Key in Loire (Chinon, Bourgueil), Bordeaux Right Bank blends, and Finger Lakes.

cards.front: What is Semillon's role in winemaking?
cards.back: Key white grape in Bordeaux. Blended with Sauvignon Blanc for dry whites (Graves) and sweet wines (Sauternes) due to its susceptibility to botrytis. Also important in Hunter Valley (Australia) for unwooded, age-worthy dry whites.

cards.front: What are the key characteristics of Zinfandel?
cards.back: Raspberry, blackberry, black pepper, bramble, jam. Medium to full body, high alcohol potential. California's heritage grape. Genetically identical to Primitivo (Italy) and Tribidrag (Croatia).

cards.front: What is Petit Verdot used for?
cards.back: A minor Bordeaux blending grape adding color, tannin, and violet/spice notes. Rarely exceeds 5-10% in Medoc blends. Increasingly used as a varietal in warm climates (Australia, Spain, Virginia).

cards.front: What is Marsanne and Roussanne?
cards.back: White Rhone grapes often blended together. Marsanne: full-bodied, nutty, low acidity. Roussanne: more aromatic, floral, herbal, higher acidity. Key in Hermitage, Crozes-Hermitage, Saint-Joseph (white).

cards.front: What is Muscat, and what are its main subtypes?
cards.back: An ancient aromatic grape family. Key types: Muscat Blanc a Petits Grains (finest, used in Asti, Beaumes-de-Venise), Muscat of Alexandria (table grape and wine), and Muscat Ottonel (Alsace).

cards.front: What is Pinot Gris/Grigio?
cards.back: Same grape, different styles. Pinot Grigio (Italy): light, crisp, neutral. Pinot Gris (Alsace): richer, more aromatic, fuller body. Also significant in Oregon (balanced, medium style).

cards.front: What grape is Torrontes?
cards.back: Argentina's signature aromatic white grape. Likely a natural cross of Muscat of Alexandria and Criolla Chica. Produces floral, aromatic dry wines, especially in Salta's high-altitude vineyards.

cards.front: What is the process of alcoholic fermentation?
cards.back: Yeast (typically Saccharomyces cerevisiae) converts grape sugars (glucose and fructose) to ethanol and CO2. Approximately 17 grams of sugar per liter yields 1% ABV.

cards.front: What is malolactic fermentation (MLF)?
cards.back: A secondary fermentation where bacteria (Oenococcus oeni) convert sharp malic acid to softer lactic acid and CO2. Softens acidity and can add buttery (diacetyl) flavors. Standard for reds; optional for whites.

cards.front: What is carbonic maceration?
cards.back: Whole, uncrushed grape clusters are placed in a CO2-filled tank. Intracellular fermentation occurs inside the berry, producing fruity, low-tannin wines. Used in Beaujolais; semi-carbonic maceration is more common.

cards.front: What is cold soaking (pre-fermentation maceration)?
cards.back: Keeping crushed grapes at low temperature (5-15C) before fermentation to extract color and fruit character from skins while minimizing tannin extraction. Common for Pinot Noir.

cards.front: What is the difference between free-run juice and press wine?
cards.back: Free-run juice drains by gravity from crushed grapes without pressing; it is generally lighter, more elegant. Press wine is extracted by pressing the remaining skins/pomace; it is darker, more tannic, more concentrated.

cards.front: Explain the traditional method (methode traditionnelle) for sparkling wine.
cards.back: Base wine undergoes secondary fermentation in the bottle. Steps: assemblage, tirage (adding yeast and sugar), secondary fermentation in bottle, aging on lees, riddling (remuage), disgorgement (degorgement), dosage, corking.

cards.front: What is the Charmat (tank) method?
cards.back: Secondary fermentation takes place in a pressurized tank rather than individual bottles. Faster, less expensive. Preserves primary fruit aromas. Used for Prosecco, Asti, Sekt, and most affordable sparkling wines.

cards.front: What is batonnage?
cards.back: Stirring the lees (spent yeast) during aging to increase mouthfeel, richness, and complexity. Common in barrel-aged Chardonnay and Muscadet sur Lie.

cards.front: What is the purpose of fining in winemaking?
cards.back: Clarifying wine by adding agents (bentonite, egg whites, casein, isinglass, gelatin) that bind with suspended particles and precipitate. Different agents target different haze-causing compounds.

cards.front: What is oak aging's effect on wine?
cards.back: Imparts flavors (vanilla, toast, coconut, spice), softens tannins through micro-oxidation, adds tannin from the wood, and allows slow oxygen exchange. French oak is typically more subtle; American oak gives stronger vanilla/coconut flavors.

cards.front: What is the difference between French and American oak?
cards.back: French oak (Quercus petraea/robur) has tighter grain, imparts subtler spice and toast. American oak (Quercus alba) has wider grain, imparts more vanilla, coconut, and dill. American oak is also less expensive.

cards.front: What is chaptalization?
cards.back: Adding sugar to grape must before fermentation to increase final alcohol level. Legal in cool regions (Burgundy, Germany, Oregon) where grapes may not fully ripen. Illegal in warm regions (e.g., southern France, California, Australia).

cards.front: What is saignee?
cards.back: Bleeding off a portion of juice from a red wine tank early in maceration. The bled-off juice can be made into rose, and the remaining red wine gains concentration. Common in Provence and Tavel.

cards.front: What is whole-cluster fermentation?
cards.back: Fermenting with intact grape stems included. Adds spice, structure, and stem tannin. Increasingly used in premium Pinot Noir (Burgundy, Oregon) and Syrah. Risk: green/herbaceous character if stems are not ripe.

cards.front: What is the appassimento method?
cards.back: Drying harvested grapes (on straw mats or in drying lofts) to concentrate sugars and flavors before fermentation. Used for Amarone della Valpolicella, Recioto, and Sforzato di Valtellina.

cards.front: What is volatile acidity (VA)?
cards.back: Acetic acid and ethyl acetate in wine. In small amounts, adds complexity. Excessive VA (vinegar-like smell, nail polish remover) is a fault. Primarily caused by Acetobacter bacteria in the presence of oxygen.

cards.front: What is Brettanomyces?
cards.back: A spoilage yeast that produces 4-ethylphenol and 4-ethylguaiacol, causing barnyard, band-aid, medicinal, and horse blanket aromas. Low levels may add complexity; high levels are considered a fault.

cards.front: What is TCA (cork taint)?
cards.back: 2,4,6-Trichloroanisole — a chemical compound that imparts musty, wet cardboard, damp basement aromas to wine. Primarily transmitted through contaminated natural cork. Detectable at very low concentrations (parts per trillion).

cards.front: What is reduction in wine?
cards.back: Off-aromas (rotten eggs, burnt rubber, struck match) from sulfur compounds, typically hydrogen sulfide, formed in low-oxygen winemaking environments. Can often be remedied by decanting or swirling; copper can bind H2S.

cards.front: What is oxidation in wine?
cards.back: Exposure to excess oxygen causing browning, loss of fruit character, and nutty/sherry-like aromas. Acetaldehyde is the key compound. Preventable with SO2, inert gas blanketing, and minimal air contact.

cards.front: What are the main roles of sulfur dioxide (SO2) in winemaking?
cards.back: Antioxidant (prevents oxidation), antimicrobial (inhibits unwanted bacteria and yeast), and binds with acetaldehyde. Added at various stages from crush to bottling.

cards.front: What is micro-oxygenation?
cards.back: Controlled introduction of tiny amounts of oxygen to wine in tank, mimicking the slow oxidation that occurs during barrel aging. Used to soften tannins and stabilize color in red wines.

cards.front: What is residual sugar (RS)?
cards.back: Unfermented grape sugar remaining in finished wine. Dry wines typically have <4 g/L RS. Off-dry: 4-12 g/L. Medium sweet: 12-45 g/L. Sweet: >45 g/L. Perception is affected by acidity level.

cards.front: What is lees aging?
cards.back: Keeping wine in contact with dead yeast cells after fermentation. Adds richness, bread/biscuit notes, creamier texture, and protects against oxidation through release of mannoproteins. Key in Champagne and Muscadet.

cards.front: What is skin contact in white winemaking?
cards.back: Allowing white grape juice to remain in contact with skins before or during fermentation. Short contact (2-24 hours) extracts aromatics. Extended contact (days to months) produces 'orange wine' with more tannin and color.

cards.front: What is the correct serving temperature for full-bodied red wines?
cards.back: 60-68F (15-20C). Serving too warm emphasizes alcohol; too cold mutes aromas and accentuates tannin.

cards.front: What is the correct serving temperature for light-bodied red wines?
cards.back: 55-60F (12-15C). Lighter reds like Beaujolais, Pinot Noir, and young Cabernet Franc benefit from slight chilling to preserve freshness.

cards.front: What is the correct serving temperature for full-bodied white wines?
cards.back: 50-55F (10-13C). Oaked Chardonnay, white Burgundy, and similar styles need slightly warmer temperatures than lighter whites to show complexity.

cards.front: What is the correct serving temperature for light-bodied and aromatic white wines?
cards.back: 45-50F (7-10C). Riesling, Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Grigio, and similar wines benefit from colder temperatures that preserve acidity and freshness.

cards.front: What is the correct serving temperature for sparkling wines?
cards.back: 40-50F (4-10C). Non-vintage sparkling at the cooler end; vintage Champagne and complex sparklers slightly warmer (45-50F) to reveal complexity.

cards.front: Describe the proper technique for opening a bottle of sparkling wine.
cards.back: Remove foil and cage (keep hand on cork). Grip the cork firmly, tilt bottle 45 degrees, and twist the bottle (not the cork) gently. Apply counter-pressure. The cork should release with a quiet sigh, not a pop.

cards.front: What is the proper order of a wine service sequence?
cards.back: Present the bottle (label facing guest), open the bottle, pour a small tasting portion for the host, await approval, then pour for guests (ladies first in traditional service, then clockwise), host last. Fill glasses one-third to one-half full.

cards.front: When should you decant a wine?
cards.back: Decant older wines to separate from sediment (pour gently, stop when sediment reaches neck). Decant young, tannic wines to aerate them and soften tannins. Pour into a clean decanter over a light source to spot sediment.

cards.front: What is the purpose of a wine cradle/basket?
cards.back: To transport aged red wine horizontally from cellar to table, minimizing disturbance of sediment. The wine is poured or decanted from the basket. It should not be used for young wines.

cards.front: What are the ideal conditions for wine storage?
cards.back: 55F (13C), 60-70% humidity, no vibration, no direct light, bottles on their side (to keep cork moist). Temperature stability is more important than exact temperature.

cards.front: What size pour is standard for a glass of wine?
cards.back: 5 ounces (150 mL) is the US standard pour. A standard 750 mL bottle yields approximately 5 glasses. Tasting pours are typically 2-3 ounces.

cards.front: What is the basic principle of food and wine pairing by weight?
cards.back: Match the weight/body of the wine with the richness/weight of the food. Light wines with light dishes; full-bodied wines with rich, heavy dishes. The wine and food should not overpower each other.

cards.front: How does acidity in wine interact with food?
cards.back: High-acid wines cut through rich, fatty, oily, or salty foods. They complement acidic foods (tomato-based dishes). Low-acid wines can taste flat alongside acidic foods.

cards.front: How does tannin in wine interact with food?
cards.back: Tannin is softened by protein and fat (e.g., steak with Cabernet Sauvignon). Salt, spice, and bitter foods exacerbate tannin's astringency. Tannic wines pair poorly with fish (metallic taste from tannin-oil interaction).

cards.front: How does sweetness in wine interact with food?
cards.back: Wine should be at least as sweet as the food; otherwise it will taste thin and acidic. Sweet wines pair with desserts, salty foods (Sauternes with Roquefort), and foie gras.

cards.front: What wine styles pair well with spicy Asian cuisine?
cards.back: Off-dry wines with low alcohol: Riesling (Kabinett/Spatlese), Gewurztraminer, off-dry Vouvray, sparkling wines. Sweetness tempers heat; high alcohol amplifies it.

cards.front: What is a classic pairing for Sauternes?
cards.back: Foie gras (sweet-fat contrast), Roquefort cheese (sweet-salty contrast), or fruit-based desserts. The wine's sweetness and acidity balance richness.

cards.front: What is the sommelier's approach to a guest who returns a wine that is not flawed?
cards.back: Graciously accept the return and offer an alternative, even if the wine is not technically flawed. Guest satisfaction takes priority. Depending on restaurant policy, the wine may be offered by the glass.

cards.front: How should a sommelier handle a corked wine?
cards.back: Confirm the TCA fault (wet cardboard, musty aroma), acknowledge it to the guest, remove the bottle, and offer a replacement. The faulty bottle should be returned to the distributor for credit.

cards.front: What is the proper way to present a wine list to guests?
cards.back: Present the wine list, offer assistance, gauge the guest's preferences and budget (take cues from price range they mention). Make recommendations that match the food order and stay within the indicated budget.

cards.front: What is the proper glassware for Champagne/sparkling wine?
cards.back: A flute preserves bubbles and focuses aromas, though tulip-shaped glasses are increasingly preferred for aged/complex Champagnes. Coupes are less ideal as they dissipate bubbles quickly.

cards.front: What is the difference between a Bordeaux glass and a Burgundy glass?
cards.back: Bordeaux glass: taller, narrower bowl directs wine to the back of the palate, ideal for tannic Cabernet. Burgundy glass: wider, rounder bowl captures delicate Pinot Noir aromas and directs wine to the tip of the tongue.

cards.front: What are the four components of systematic wine tasting (CMS deductive method)?
cards.back: Sight (color, clarity, viscosity), Nose (condition, fruit, non-fruit aromas, intensity), Palate (sweetness, acidity, tannin, body, alcohol, finish), and Conclusion (grape, region, vintage, quality).

cards.front: What does high viscosity (thick legs/tears) indicate in a wine?
cards.back: Higher alcohol and/or residual sugar content. This is the Marangoni effect (Gibbs-Marangoni), driven by the difference in surface tension between alcohol and water.

cards.front: What is Cognac, and how is it classified?
cards.back: A brandy from the Cognac region of France, double-distilled in copper pot stills from Ugni Blanc grapes. Classifications: VS (min 2 years), VSOP (min 4 years), XO (min 10 years, changed from 6 in 2018).

cards.front: What are the six Cognac crus (sub-regions)?
cards.back: Grande Champagne (finest), Petite Champagne, Borderies, Fins Bois, Bons Bois, and Bois Ordinaires. 'Fine Champagne' denotes a blend of Grande and Petite Champagne with at least 50% Grande.

cards.front: What is Armagnac, and how does it differ from Cognac?
cards.back: A brandy from Gascony, southwest France. Typically single-distilled in a column still (vs. Cognac's double pot distillation). Generally more rustic, with richer, more complex flavors. Can be vintage-dated.

cards.front: What are the five recognized Scotch whisky regions?
cards.back: Speyside, Highland, Lowland, Islay, and Campbeltown. Speyside produces the most; Islay is known for heavily peated whiskies.

cards.front: What is the difference between single malt and blended Scotch whisky?
cards.back: Single malt: 100% malted barley from a single distillery. Blended: a mix of malt and grain whiskies from multiple distilleries. Blended malt: malt whiskies from multiple distilleries (no grain whisky).

cards.front: What is the minimum aging requirement for Scotch whisky?
cards.back: Minimum 3 years in oak casks in Scotland. Any age statement must reflect the youngest whisky in the blend.

cards.front: What is Bourbon, and what are its legal requirements?
cards.back: American whiskey made from at least 51% corn, distilled to no more than 160 proof, entered into new charred oak barrels at no more than 125 proof, and bottled at minimum 80 proof. No minimum aging (but 'straight' requires 2 years).

cards.front: What distinguishes Tennessee whiskey from Bourbon?
cards.back: Tennessee whiskey (e.g., Jack Daniel's) undergoes the Lincoln County Process: filtering through sugar maple charcoal before aging. Otherwise meets all Bourbon requirements.

cards.front: What is Rye whiskey?
cards.back: American rye must be made from at least 51% rye grain and follow the same proof/barrel requirements as Bourbon (new charred oak). Canadian whisky is often called 'rye' but has different regulations.

cards.front: What are the three categories of Tequila based on aging?
cards.back: Blanco/Silver (unaged or <2 months), Reposado (2-12 months in oak), Anejo (1-3 years in oak). Extra Anejo: 3+ years. Must be made from 100% blue Weber agave in designated Mexican regions (primarily Jalisco).

cards.front: What is the difference between Tequila and Mezcal?
cards.back: Tequila is a type of Mezcal made only from blue Weber agave in designated regions. Mezcal can be made from 30+ agave species, primarily in Oaxaca. Mezcal often has a smoky character from roasting agave in earthen pits.

cards.front: What are the main botanicals in gin?
cards.back: Juniper berries must be the dominant flavor. Common additional botanicals: coriander, angelica root, citrus peel, orris root, licorice, cardamom. London Dry Gin cannot have added flavors after distillation.

cards.front: What are the main styles of gin?
cards.back: London Dry (no added sugar or flavors post-distillation), Plymouth (slightly sweeter, from Plymouth, England), Old Tom (sweeter, historical style), Genever/Jenever (Dutch/Belgian, malt wine base, the precursor to modern gin).

cards.front: What is Vodka made from?
cards.back: Can be made from virtually any fermentable material: grain (wheat, rye, corn), potato, grape, or other. Must be distilled to at least 190 proof (US) and bottled at minimum 80 proof. Should be neutral in character.

cards.front: What are the main styles of Rum?
cards.back: White/Silver (unaged or filtered), Gold (lightly aged), Dark (longer aging), Aged/Anejo (premium, longer barrel time), Overproof (higher ABV), Rhum Agricole (made from fresh sugarcane juice, not molasses, in French Caribbean).

cards.front: What is Rhum Agricole?
cards.back: Rum made from fresh-pressed sugarcane juice (not molasses) in the French Caribbean, especially Martinique (AOC). Has a grassy, vegetal character. Martinique has its own AOC classification.

cards.front: What is Pisco?
cards.back: An unaged grape brandy from Peru or Chile. Peruvian Pisco cannot be diluted after distillation and must be made from specific grape varieties. Chilean Pisco allows dilution and oak aging. Key cocktail: Pisco Sour.

cards.front: What is Grappa?
cards.back: An Italian pomace brandy distilled from grape skins, seeds, and stems left over after winemaking. Can be unaged (bianca) or aged (invecchiata, stravecchia). Quality ranges widely.

cards.front: What is Calvados?
cards.back: An apple (and sometimes pear) brandy from Normandy, France. AOC regulated. Calvados Pays d'Auge must be double pot-distilled. Aging: Fine (2+ years), VSOP (4+ years), XO (6+ years).

cards.front: What is Chartreuse?
cards.back: A French herbal liqueur made by Carthusian monks since 1737. Green Chartreuse is 55% ABV with 130 botanical ingredients. Yellow Chartreuse is milder at 40% ABV. Only liqueur with a naturally green color.

cards.front: What are the main categories of liqueurs?
cards.back: Herbal/botanical (Chartreuse, Benedictine), fruit (Cointreau, Grand Marnier, Chambord), nut (Amaretto, Frangelico), cream (Baileys), coffee (Kahlua), and anise (Pastis, Sambuca, Absinthe).

cards.front: What is Absinthe?
cards.back: A high-proof anise-flavored spirit distilled with wormwood (Artemisia absinthium), green anise, and fennel. Traditionally diluted with water through a sugar cube, causing louche (clouding). Not actually hallucinogenic.

cards.front: What is Vermouth?
cards.back: An aromatized, fortified wine flavored with botanicals (wormwood is essential — 'Wermut' is German for wormwood). Styles: Dry/French (pale, dry), Sweet/Italian (red, sweet), Blanc/Bianco (sweet, pale). Key in Martini and Manhattan cocktails.

cards.front: What is Sherry's alcohol range after fortification?
cards.back: Fino/Manzanilla: 15-15.5% (fortified to allow flor growth). Oloroso: 17-22% (fortified above flor threshold). Amontillado starts as Fino, then is further fortified to stop flor.

cards.front: What is Port wine?
cards.back: A fortified wine from the Douro Valley, Portugal. Grape spirit (aguardente at 77% ABV) is added during fermentation to stop it, leaving residual sugar. Styles: Ruby, Tawny, Vintage, Late Bottled Vintage (LBV), Colheita, Vintage/Vintage Port.

cards.front: What are the main styles of Port?
cards.back: Ruby (young, fruity), Reserve Ruby, Late Bottled Vintage/LBV (4-6 years in barrel), Vintage/Vintage Port (declared vintage, 2-3 years cask, ages in bottle decades), Tawny (aged in cask), 10/20/30/40-Year Tawny, Colheita (single vintage tawny), White Port.

cards.front: What is Madeira, and what are its styles?
cards.back: A fortified wine from the island of Madeira, Portugal. Heated (estufagem) during aging. Styles by grape (driest to sweetest): Sercial (dry), Verdelho (off-dry), Bual/Boal (medium sweet), Malmsey/Malvasia (sweet). Nearly indestructible — can age for centuries.

cards.front: What are the main styles of beer by fermentation type?
cards.back: Ale (top-fermenting yeast, Saccharomyces cerevisiae, warmer fermentation 15-24C) and Lager (bottom-fermenting yeast, Saccharomyces pastorianus, cooler fermentation 7-13C). Spontaneous fermentation (Lambic) uses wild yeast.

cards.front: What is an IPA (India Pale Ale)?
cards.back: A hoppy ale style originally brewed in England for export to India. Characterized by high hop bitterness, floral/citrus/pine aromas, and moderate to high alcohol. Sub-styles: West Coast (bitter, dry), New England/Hazy (juicy, low bitterness), Double/Imperial.

cards.front: What is a Pilsner?
cards.back: A pale lager originating from Pilsen, Czech Republic (1842). Czech/Bohemian Pilsner: Saaz hops, soft water, bready malt. German Pilsner: drier, more bitter, crisper. The most widely consumed beer style globally.

cards.front: What is a Stout?
cards.back: A dark, top-fermented ale using roasted barley or malt. Sub-styles: Dry/Irish Stout (Guinness-style, dry, roasty), Oatmeal Stout (smooth, creamy), Milk/Sweet Stout (lactose added), Imperial/Russian Stout (high ABV, intense).

cards.front: What is a Belgian Tripel?
cards.back: A strong, pale Belgian ale (7-10% ABV) with fruity esters (banana, pear), spicy phenolics (clove, pepper), and a dry finish. Traditionally brewed by Trappist monasteries. The 'triple' referred to the amount of malt used.

cards.front: What is a Hefeweizen?
cards.back: A Bavarian wheat beer made with at least 50% wheat malt and top-fermented. Unfiltered (cloudy). Characteristic banana (isoamyl acetate) and clove (4-vinylguaiacol) aromas from the yeast.

cards.front: What is a Saison?
cards.back: A Belgian/French farmhouse ale, originally brewed for seasonal workers. Dry, effervescent, fruity, peppery, with moderate to high carbonation. ABV typically 5-8%. Uses distinctive Saison yeast strains.

cards.front: What is a Lambic?
cards.back: A Belgian wheat beer fermented spontaneously with wild yeast (Brettanomyces) and bacteria in the Pajottenland region near Brussels. Sour, funky, dry. Gueuze is a blend of young and old Lambics; Kriek adds cherries.

cards.front: What is a Gose?
cards.back: A German sour wheat beer from Goslar/Leipzig. Brewed with coriander and salt, lightly tart from Lactobacillus fermentation. Low ABV (4-5%), refreshing, slightly saline.

cards.front: What is a Porter?
cards.back: A dark ale brewed with roasted brown malt. Originated in 18th-century London. Chocolate, caramel, coffee flavors. Lighter bodied than Stout. Sub-styles: English Porter, American Porter, Baltic Porter (lager yeast, higher ABV).

cards.front: What are the four main ingredients in beer?
cards.back: Water, malt (usually barley), hops (for bitterness, flavor, aroma, and preservation), and yeast. The Reinheitsgebot (1516 Bavarian Purity Law) originally permitted only water, barley, and hops.

cards.front: What does IBU measure in beer?
cards.back: International Bitterness Units — a measurement of hop-derived iso-alpha acids. Higher IBU = more bitter. Light lagers: 5-15 IBU. Pale Ale: 30-50 IBU. IPA: 40-70+ IBU. Perception is also affected by malt sweetness.

cards.front: What is sake, and what is it made from?
cards.back: A Japanese alcoholic beverage brewed from polished rice, water, koji mold (Aspergillus oryzae), and yeast. Not a wine or beer — the koji converts starch to sugar simultaneously with fermentation (multiple parallel fermentation).

cards.front: What is the rice polishing ratio (seimaibuai) in sake?
cards.back: The percentage of the rice grain remaining after polishing. Lower percentage = more polished = higher grade. Daiginjo: 50% or less remaining. Ginjo: 60% or less. Honjozo/Junmai: 70% or less (though Junmai has no legal minimum).

cards.front: What are the main sake classifications?
cards.back: Junmai (pure rice, no added alcohol), Honjozo (small amount of brewers' alcohol added), Ginjo (60% polish or less), Daiginjo (50% polish or less). Junmai Ginjo/Daiginjo = Ginjo/Daiginjo with no added alcohol.

cards.front: What does 'Junmai' mean on a sake label?
cards.back: 'Pure rice' — no brewers' alcohol added. Only rice, water, koji, and yeast. Tends to be fuller-bodied and more rice-forward compared to non-Junmai styles.

cards.front: What is the role of koji in sake production?
cards.back: Koji (Aspergillus oryzae) is a mold cultivated on steamed rice that produces enzymes to convert rice starch into fermentable sugars. This is essential because rice has no natural enzymes for saccharification.

cards.front: What is Nigori sake?
cards.back: Coarsely filtered or unfiltered sake, retaining rice sediment. Appears cloudy/milky. Often sweeter and creamier than clear sake. Served chilled.

cards.front: What is the ideal serving temperature for premium Ginjo/Daiginjo sake?
cards.back: Chilled, around 45-50F (7-10C), to preserve delicate floral and fruity aromatics. Junmai and Honjozo can be served at a wider range of temperatures, including warm (100-120F/40-50C).

cards.front: What is Nama sake?
cards.back: Unpasteurized sake (most sake is pasteurized twice). Must be kept refrigerated. Has a fresh, lively character with more vibrant aromatics.

cards.front: What is a Belgian Dubbel?
cards.back: A dark, malty, moderately strong Trappist-style ale (6-7.5% ABV). Dark fruit (raisin, plum), caramel, and spicy yeast character. Traditionally brewed in Trappist monasteries.

cards.front: What is a Bock beer?
cards.back: A strong German lager (6-7% ABV) with rich malty sweetness. Sub-styles: Maibock/Helles Bock (paler, spring), Doppelbock (stronger, 7-12%, names often end in '-ator'), Eisbock (freeze-concentrated).

cards.front: What is a Sour Ale?
cards.back: A beer style intentionally brewed with acid-producing bacteria (Lactobacillus, Pediococcus) and/or wild yeast (Brettanomyces). Traditional examples: Lambic, Gueuze, Flanders Red, Berliner Weisse. Modern: kettle sours.

cards.front: What is a Trappist beer?
cards.back: Beer brewed within a Trappist monastery, under monk supervision, with profits supporting the monastery and charity. Currently 14 recognized Trappist breweries worldwide. Famous: Chimay, Westmalle, Orval, Westvleteren, Rochefort.

cards.front: What is Shochu?
cards.back: A Japanese distilled spirit (typically 25-35% ABV) made from barley, sweet potato, rice, buckwheat, or brown sugar. Single distilled (honkaku) or multiple distilled (korui). Not to be confused with sake.

cards.front: What is the difference between Sparkling Sake and traditional sparkling wine?
cards.back: Sparkling sake gets its carbonation from in-bottle secondary fermentation of rice or from forced carbonation. Generally lower alcohol (5-12%) than sparkling wine. Served chilled in smaller portions.

cards.front: What is a Witbier/Biere Blanche?
cards.back: A Belgian wheat beer brewed with unmalted wheat, coriander, and Curacao orange peel. Cloudy, light, refreshing, citrusy. Typically 4.5-5.5% ABV. Hoegaarden is the benchmark example.

cards.front: What is Koshu sake?
cards.back: Aged sake, typically amber-colored with nutty, caramel, and umami-rich flavors. Can be aged for years or decades. Pairs well with rich, savory dishes.

cards.front: What grapes make up a typical GSM blend?
cards.back: Grenache, Syrah, and Mourvedre. The classic Southern Rhone combination (Chateauneuf-du-Pape) also widely used in Australia (Barossa), South Africa, and California.

cards.front: What is Albarino's origin and flavor profile?
cards.back: A white grape from Galicia (NW Spain) and northern Portugal (as Alvarinho in Vinho Verde). Stone fruit, citrus, saline/mineral quality, high acidity. Usually unoaked.

cards.front: What is Assyrtiko?
cards.back: A Greek white grape, most famously grown on the volcanic island of Santorini. High acidity, mineral, citrus, and saline character even in warm climates. Vines are trained in basket shapes (kouloura) for wind protection.

cards.front: What is Tannat?
cards.back: An extremely tannic red grape from Madiran (SW France). Also the signature grape of Uruguay, where it produces slightly softer wines. Name derives from the high tannin content.

cards.front: What is Cinsault?
cards.back: A red Mediterranean grape used in blending throughout southern France (Rhone, Languedoc, Provence). One parent of Pinotage (crossed with Pinot Noir). Produces light, fruity, low-tannin wines.

cards.front: What are the principal white grapes of Bordeaux?
cards.back: Sauvignon Blanc and Semillon (primary), with Muscadelle as a minor blending partner. Dry whites of Graves/Pessac-Leognan blend SB and Sem; sweet Sauternes favors Semillon for its botrytis susceptibility.

cards.front: What is Melon de Bourgogne?
cards.back: The sole grape of Muscadet (Loire Valley). Produces neutral, light, high-acid, mineral-driven whites. Originally from Burgundy, planted in the Loire after a devastating frost in 1709.

cards.front: What is Verdejo?
cards.back: A Spanish white grape from Rueda DO (Castilla y Leon). Produces aromatic wines with fennel, citrus, and herbal notes. High acidity. Often compared to Sauvignon Blanc.

cards.front: What is Carmenere?
cards.back: A red Bordeaux variety nearly extinct in France after phylloxera. Rediscovered in Chile in 1994 (originally misidentified as Merlot). Produces medium-bodied wines with green pepper, dark fruit, and smoky notes.

cards.front: What is the concept of terroir?
cards.back: The complete natural environment in which wine is produced: soil, subsoil, climate (macro and micro), aspect, altitude, and drainage. Terroir expresses the unique character of a place in the resulting wine.

cards.front: What is phylloxera?
cards.back: Dactylosphaera vitifoliae — a root-feeding aphid that devastated European vineyards in the late 19th century. The solution was grafting Vitis vinifera vines onto resistant American rootstocks. Some regions (Chile, parts of Australia, old-vine plantings in sandy soils) remain ungrafted.

cards.front: What is the difference between organic and biodynamic viticulture?
cards.back: Organic prohibits synthetic chemicals (pesticides, herbicides, fungicides). Biodynamic follows Rudolf Steiner's principles: organic practices plus specific preparations (horn manure, horn silica) and a cosmic calendar for vineyard activities.

cards.front: What is green harvesting (vendange verte)?
cards.back: Removing unripe grape clusters during the growing season to reduce yields and concentrate flavor and energy into the remaining fruit. Typically done at veraison (color change).

cards.front: What is veraison?
cards.back: The onset of grape ripening, marked by color change (green to purple for reds, green to golden for whites), sugar accumulation, acid reduction, and softening. Occurs mid to late summer.

cards.front: What is botrytis cinerea (noble rot)?
cards.back: A fungus that, under specific humid-then-dry conditions, desiccates grapes and concentrates sugars, acids, and glycerol. Essential for Sauternes, Tokaji Aszu, Beerenauslese/TBA, and some Loire sweet wines. Uncontrolled, it becomes destructive gray rot.

cards.front: What is the Denominacion de Origen Calificada (DOCa) for Rioja?
cards.back: Rioja received DOCa status in 1991, the first Spanish region to do so. This highest classification requires stricter production controls, including mandatory bottling within the region.

cards.front: What is the traditional winemaking approach of Rioja vs. the modern style?
cards.back: Traditional: long aging in American oak, lighter extraction, Tempranillo-Garnacha blends. Modern: shorter aging in French oak, deeper extraction, sometimes single-varietal Tempranillo. Both are valid DOCa approaches.

cards.front: What is Toro DO known for?
cards.back: A DO in Castilla y Leon, Spain. Known for powerful, full-bodied reds from Tinta de Toro (a local clone of Tempranillo). Hot, dry climate produces deeply concentrated wines.

cards.front: What is the Charmat method also known as?
cards.back: Metodo Italiano, Martinotti method (after its Italian inventor), or cuve close. The secondary fermentation occurs in a pressurized stainless steel tank rather than in individual bottles.

cards.front: What is a negociant in Burgundy?
cards.back: A merchant who buys grapes, juice, or finished wines from growers and bottles under their own label. Major negociants: Louis Jadot, Joseph Drouhin, Bouchard Pere et Fils. A domaine is an estate that grows and bottles its own wine.

cards.front: What is a monopole in Burgundy?
cards.back: A vineyard owned entirely by a single producer. Examples: Romanee-Conti (Domaine de la Romanee-Conti), La Tache (DRC), Clos de Tart (owned by Francois Pinault), La Romanee (Domaine du Comte Liger-Belair).

cards.front: What is the climat system in Burgundy?
cards.back: A climat is a precisely defined vineyard parcel with unique soil, exposure, and microclimate characteristics. Burgundy's climats were added to the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2015. There are over 1,200 recognized climats.

cards.front: What is Cremant?
cards.back: French traditional method sparkling wine produced outside Champagne. Key appellations: Cremant d'Alsace, Cremant de Bourgogne, Cremant de Loire, Cremant de Limoux, Cremant de Bordeaux, Cremant du Jura.

cards.front: What is Tavel?
cards.back: The only AOC in France exclusively dedicated to rose wine, located in the Southern Rhone Valley. Made primarily from Grenache and Cinsault. Known for full-bodied, structured rose.

cards.front: What is the Languedoc-Roussillon known for?
cards.back: The largest wine-producing region in France (and the world by volume). Produces a wide range of styles. Key AOCs: Corbieres, Minervois, Faugeres, Pic Saint-Loup, Fitou. Important IGP area (Pays d'Oc).

cards.front: What is Bandol known for?
cards.back: An AOC in Provence specializing in Mourvedre-based reds (min 50%) and notable rose. Red wines must age 18 months in oak. Warm Mediterranean climate, hillside vineyards.

cards.front: What is Vin Jaune?
cards.back: A specialty of the Jura region (eastern France), made from Savagnin grape. Aged under a voile (film of yeast similar to flor) for a minimum of 6 years and 3 months. Bottled in a 62 cl clavelin. Oxidative, nutty, curry-spice character.

cards.front: What is Tokaji Aszu?
cards.back: A sweet wine from the Tokaj region of Hungary. Made by adding botrytized grape paste (aszu berries) to a base wine. Sweetness historically measured in puttonyos (3-6). Made primarily from Furmint with Harslevelu and Sarga Muskotaly.

cards.front: What is Zweigelt?
cards.back: Austria's most widely planted red grape, a cross of Blaufrankisch and St. Laurent. Produces medium-bodied, fruity, cherry-driven reds. Created by Fritz Zweigelt in 1922.

cards.front: What grape is Barossa Valley Shiraz known for?
cards.back: Syrah/Shiraz, producing bold, rich, full-bodied wines with dark fruit, chocolate, and spice. Many old-vine (100+ years) vineyards survived because phylloxera did not reach the Barossa.

cards.front: What is the Margaret River region known for?
cards.back: A premium region in Western Australia known for Bordeaux-style blends (Cabernet-Merlot) and Chardonnay. Maritime climate moderated by the Indian and Southern Oceans.

cards.front: What is the classification system for Bordeaux's Graves region?
cards.back: The Classification of Graves (1953, updated 1959). Classifies chateaux as 'Crus Classes de Graves' for red and/or white wines. Chateau Haut-Brion is classified in both the 1855 Medoc and Graves classifications.

cards.front: What is Valpolicella DOC?
cards.back: A DOC in Veneto (northeast Italy) producing red wines from Corvina, Corvinone, and Rondinella. Basic Valpolicella is light and fruity. Superiore requires higher alcohol and 1 year aging. Classico denotes the original zone.

cards.front: What is Recioto della Valpolicella?
cards.back: A sweet DOCG wine from Valpolicella made using the appassimento method (dried grapes). Unlike Amarone, fermentation is arrested before all sugar converts, leaving a sweet wine.

cards.front: What is Barbaresco's minimum aging and where are its key communes?
cards.back: Minimum 26 months total (9 in wood), Riserva 50 months. Key communes: Barbaresco, Neive, Treiso, and San Rocco Seno d'Elvio.

cards.front: What is Gavi (Cortese di Gavi)?
cards.back: A DOCG white wine from Piedmont made from 100% Cortese grape. Produces crisp, mineral, citrus-driven whites. One of Piedmont's most important white wines.

cards.front: What is Vernaccia di San Gimignano?
cards.back: A DOCG white wine from Tuscany, made from Vernaccia grape. It was the first Italian DOC wine (1966). Dry, crisp, almond-noted whites from the hilltop town of San Gimignano.

cards.front: What is Vermentino, and where is it grown in Italy?
cards.back: A white Mediterranean grape grown in Sardinia (Vermentino di Gallura DOCG), Liguria, and Tuscany. Produces aromatic, herbal, citrus whites. Also grown in southern France as Rolle.

cards.front: What is Nero d'Avola?
cards.back: Sicily's most important red grape. Produces full-bodied, fruity wines with plum, black cherry, and spice. Named after the town of Avola in southeast Sicily.

cards.front: What is Fiano di Avellino?
cards.back: A DOCG white wine from Campania (southern Italy) made from Fiano. Produces complex, aromatic whites with hazelnut, pear, and honey notes. One of southern Italy's finest whites.

cards.front: What is the difference between Cru Bourgeois and Cru Classe in Bordeaux?
cards.back: Cru Classe refers to the 1855 classified growths (61 chateaux in the Medoc + Haut-Brion). Cru Bourgeois is a lower tier for Medoc chateaux not included in 1855, revised periodically (most recently with a new three-tier system in 2020).

cards.front: What is En Primeur in Bordeaux?
cards.back: The futures system where wines are sold while still in barrel, typically in the spring following the vintage. Buyers pay in advance for delivery 18-24 months later. Allows early access to sought-after wines.

cards.front: What are the key villages of Cote de Beaune for white wine?
cards.back: Meursault, Puligny-Montrachet, and Chassagne-Montrachet. These produce Burgundy's greatest Chardonnays, including Grand Crus Montrachet, Chevalier-Montrachet, and Batard-Montrachet.

cards.front: What is Pouilly-Fuisse?
cards.back: The top appellation of the Maconnais (southern Burgundy) for Chardonnay. Received Premier Cru status in 2020. Fuller-bodied than Chablis, often with some oak influence.

cards.front: What are the key differences between Chablis and Cote de Beaune Chardonnay?
cards.back: Chablis is typically unoaked or lightly oaked, with steely acidity and mineral character from Kimmeridgian soils. Cote de Beaune (Meursault, Puligny) is usually barrel-fermented/aged, with richer, more opulent character.

cards.front: What is Hermitage known for?
cards.back: A prestigious Northern Rhone AOC producing powerful, age-worthy Syrah reds and small amounts of white (Marsanne/Roussanne). The granite hill of Hermitage overlooks the town of Tain-l'Hermitage.

cards.front: What is Crozes-Hermitage?
cards.back: The largest appellation in the Northern Rhone, surrounding Hermitage. Produces Syrah-based reds and Marsanne/Roussanne whites. Generally more approachable and less concentrated than Hermitage.

cards.front: What is Gigondas?
cards.back: A Southern Rhone cru producing Grenache-based reds (often with Syrah and Mourvedre). Sometimes called 'the poor man's Chateauneuf-du-Pape.' Full-bodied, spicy, garrigue-scented wines.

cards.front: What is the role of the Chef de Cave in Champagne?
cards.back: The cellar master responsible for blending the house style, maintaining consistency across vintages through reserve wines, and deciding whether to declare a vintage. A critical role in non-vintage Champagne production.

cards.front: What is the reserve wine system in Champagne?
cards.back: Wines from previous vintages held back and blended into non-vintage (NV) Champagne to maintain house style consistency. The proportion of reserve wine varies by house and can significantly affect quality and complexity.

cards.front: What is Grower Champagne?
cards.back: Champagne made by the grape grower (Recoltant Manipulant, or RM on the label) rather than a large house (Negociant Manipulant, NM). Increasingly popular for their terroir-driven, individual character.

cards.front: What is the Anderson Valley AVA known for?
cards.back: A cool-climate AVA in Mendocino County, California. Known for Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and high-quality sparkling wine (Roederer Estate). Marine fog funnels through the Navarro River gap.

cards.front: What is the Central Otago wine region?
cards.back: The world's southernmost wine region, located on New Zealand's South Island. Known for concentrated, aromatic Pinot Noir grown at high elevations with continental climate and dramatic diurnal temperature variation.

cards.front: What is the Uco Valley in Argentina?
cards.back: A high-elevation (900-1,500 meters) sub-region of Mendoza producing increasingly prestigious Malbec and other varieties. Key areas: Tupungato, Tunuyan, San Carlos. Altitude provides UV intensity and diurnal temperature shifts.

cards.front: What is the Casablanca Valley?
cards.back: A cool-climate wine region in Chile, near the coast. Known for Sauvignon Blanc, Chardonnay, and Pinot Noir. Cooling Humboldt Current influence and morning fog.

cards.front: What is the Clare Valley known for?
cards.back: A premium South Australian region famous for dry Riesling, typically sealed with screwcaps. The wines are lime-driven, steely, and age well, developing toast and petrol notes.

cards.front: What is the Hunter Valley known for?
cards.back: New South Wales, Australia. Known for Semillon (unwooded, low-alcohol, age-worthy) and Shiraz. Hot, humid climate moderated by afternoon cloud cover. Some of Australia's oldest wine estates.

cards.front: What is the Constantia region of South Africa known for?
cards.back: South Africa's oldest wine region (est. 1685), in Cape Town. Historically famous for Vin de Constance, a sweet Muscat dessert wine. Today known for Sauvignon Blanc and cool-climate styles.

cards.front: What is the Walla Walla AVA?
cards.back: A premium AVA spanning Washington and Oregon. Known for Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Syrah. Loess (wind-blown) soils over basalt bedrock. Continental climate with warm days and cool nights.

cards.front: What is the Maipo Valley known for?
cards.back: Chile's most prestigious red wine region, near Santiago. Produces top Cabernet Sauvignon, particularly from Alto Maipo (higher elevations in the Andes foothills).

cards.front: What is Vinho Verde?
cards.back: A DOC in northwest Portugal producing light, crisp, often slightly effervescent white wines. Key grapes: Alvarinho (Albarino), Loureiro, Trajadura. Typically low alcohol, high acidity, consumed young.

cards.front: What is the Douro Valley's dual identity?
cards.back: The Douro produces both Port (fortified wine from the demarcated region) and increasingly prestigious unfortified dry red wines (Douro DOC) from the same grape varieties: Touriga Nacional, Touriga Franca, Tinta Roriz, etc.

cards.front: What is the Willamette Valley's climate classification?
cards.back: A cool, maritime-influenced climate (Region I on the Winkler scale). Warm, dry summers with long, mild autumns. Similar latitude to Burgundy. Annual rainfall primarily falls in winter.

cards.front: What is the difference between American and European approaches to labeling wine?
cards.back: European (Old World): wines are typically labeled by region/appellation (e.g., Chablis, Barolo). New World: wines are typically labeled by grape variety (e.g., Chardonnay, Cabernet Sauvignon). This distinction is increasingly blurred.
